These impressive bees range from western Texas all the way to northern California and the Hawaiian islands, making them one of the most widely distributed carpenter bees in North America.

The males' luminous green eyes are particularly eye-catching when they're out foraging, and they cut a very different figure from their dark-bodied female counterparts.

Myrmecia pilosula.summary:
This venomous Australian ant is a champion jumper, capable of launching itself several times its own body length to escape danger or chase down prey. Despite their jumping prowess, they're not tiny — workers measure 12-14 mm long, with queens reaching 14-16 mm, making them quite substantial insects for Australia.

Found primarily in Tasmania and southeast Australia, these ants are famous enough to have earned multiple common names: jack jumper, jumping jack, hopper ant, and jumper ant all refer to the same impressive acrobat.

Camponotus intrepidus.summary:
These ants belong to a genus boasting over 1,500 described species — making it one of the most successful ant groups on the planet, found nearly everywhere except Antarctica. Here's where it gets delightfully funny: despite being called 'carpenter ants,' the vast majority don't actually nest in wood.

Only a few specialized subgroups have the wood-boring habit, meaning most of these ants are just regular ground or rock dwellers sporting a gloriously misleading name that's stuck around for centuries. It's nature's longest-running case of mistaken identity.

Dolichoderus scrobiculatus.summary:
This Australian ant earned its distinctive 'sharkfinned' nickname from something visually or behaviorally striking — one of those delightful quirks of entomological naming that rewards curiosity. Endemic to Australia and formally described back in 1876, this species has been part of the region's ant fauna for well over a century.

The scientific name 'scrobiculatus' hints at its textured or pitted surface, suggesting these ants have notable surface details that make them visually distinctive among Australia's remarkably diverse ant roster.

Camponotus consobrinus.summary:
Watch these ants follow a sugar trail and you'll instantly understand their common name: they're shameless sugar lovers, drawn to sweet food with almost comedic eagerness that makes them frequent kitchen invaders. Crowned with a distinctive orange-brown band across their gaster (rear end), these Australian ants make themselves instantly recognizable.

First described by entomologist Erichson way back in 1842, they've been delighting (or frustrating) Australians ever since with their relentless quest for sweet treats and their photogenic appearance.

Opisthopsis rufithorax.summary:
These ants are part of a genus containing just 13 species, all confined to Australasia — making them a truly exclusive club in the ant world. The 'strobe' in their common name likely hints at rapid, darting movements or flashing behavior that makes them difficult to track even when you're watching carefully.

Their extreme regional specificity shows how ant diversity can be hyper-localized, with entire genera found in just one part of the world, representing a unique and specialized slice of Australasian entomology.

Myrmecia tarsata.summary:
Another entry in Australia's impressive roster of jumping bull ants, this species makes its home primarily in New South Wales and the Australian Capital Territory. These impressive insects combine considerable size with aggressive attitude, and their jumping ability gives them an extra tactical advantage for hunting or rapid escape.

The bull ant name covers Australia's most fearsome ant group — known for their venom, their size, and their willingness to use both when provoked. They're the kind of neighbor that earns respect through sheer force of personality.

Vespa tropica.summary: Recently discovered as an invasive species on the Pacific island of Guam, this tropical hornet is spreading far beyond its native range in Southern Asia and West Africa. The greater banded hornet is a formidable predator—it actively hunts paper wasps—and brings serious muscle with an extraordinarily potent sting that causes extreme pain and swelling. Its expanding presence in new territories has authorities rightfully concerned about impact on native ecosystems.

Helophilus fasciatus.summary: These abundant flower flies have mastered aerial acrobatics. They can hover nearly motionless in mid-air, a talent that makes them efficient flower visitors and pollinators. Adults lay eggs on vegetation overhanging water, and when the larvae hatch, they simply drop into the water below. The larvae thrive in murky, oxygen-poor worlds of decaying vegetation and farmyard manure—environments that would be lethal to most insects.

Diogmites platypterus.summary: Aptly named the Black Hanging Thief, this robber fly lives up to its fearsome reputation. These aerial ambush predators hang in place and wait for unsuspecting prey to fly past, then strike with lightning reflexes. Robber flies are among nature's most formidable insect hunters, equipped with incredible vision and hunting prowess. This sleek predator rules the skies with precision, snatching dragonflies, mosquitoes, and other insects mid-flight. Watch carefully in sunny spots—a hunt may unfold before your eyes.

Eristalis dimidiata.summary: This forest-dwelling hoverfly is a champion of endurance. It flies year-round in warmer regions, defying winter shutdowns, and is one of the earliest hoverflies to appear in spring. Come late March in northern areas, while most insects slumber, this hardy fly is already visiting flowers. It likely overwinters as an adult, sheltering in leaf litter or tree bark, then awakening at the first warm spell. This early emergence strategy gives it access to early-season flowers and minimal competition.

Sericomyia flagrans.summary: Native to North America, this flower fly is a familiar visitor to blooms throughout the continent. The distinctive spotted wing pattern gives it its common name, making it visually distinctive among its hoverfly relatives. As an adult, it feeds on nectar and pollen while simultaneously serving as a pollinator. These elegant hoverers contribute significantly to pollination while displaying their graceful, motionless flight. Watch for them dancing around wildflowers during warm months.

Pyrgota undata.summary: Don't let the delicate common name fool you—these waved light flies lead a sinister double life. Pyrgotidae flies are parasites of other insects, particularly beetles, employing a strategy of deception and biological warfare. Females lay eggs on unsuspecting hosts, and when the larvae hatch, they develop inside, hijacking the body of another creature. It's a gruesome but effective survival strategy that plays out invisibly in the insect world. These seemingly innocent flies are nature's stealth assassins.

Toxomerus geminatus.summary: Tiny but mighty: at just 6 to 7 millimeters long, the Eastern Calligrapher packs outsized impact. Its larvae are fierce predators of aphids and mites, making it a natural pest-control powerhouse. The name 'Calligrapher' hints at beautiful markings adorning its body—elegant patterns that make it a lovely garden visitor. While the adult hovers delicately around flowers sipping nectar and pollen, its offspring are working as beneficial insects. For gardeners, this small fly's presence is proof that the best pest control comes from nature.

Episyrphus balteatus.summary: This delightfully named hoverfly lives up to its moniker with striking orange-and-black coloring that echoes marmalade. It's the most abundant native hoverfly in Central Europe, a true ecological success story. At just 9 to 12 millimeters, it may be small, but its widespread distribution across Europe, North Asia, and North Africa tells the real story—these flies have successfully adapted to diverse habitats. Watch for their distinctive coloring at flowers during warm months.

Eristalis pertinax.summary: This hoverfly's larvae are ultimate survivors of grotesque habitats. The rat-tailed maggots thrive in drainage ditches, sewage, and manure piles—environments so polluted and oxygen-poor that most aquatic creatures would perish. The 'rat tail' is actually a breathing tube that allows larvae to extract oxygen from stagnant water. First described in 1763, these tapered drone flies have been nature's cleanup crew for centuries. Found across Europe and Asia, they're a testament to adaptation, turning humanity's waste into their triumph.

Volucella pellucens.summary: The name says it all: pellucid means transparent and clear, and this distinctive hoverfly's body and wings exemplify that elegant quality. Its delicate, translucent appearance sets it apart from more opaque relatives, giving it a gossamer-like beauty. Like all hoverflies, it visits flowers for nectar and pollen, contributing to pollination. Watch for this ethereal fly near blooms during warm months, hanging motionless in the air before darting to the next flower. Its transparent wings catch the light beautifully, making it a delicate gem of the insect world.

Aedes geniculatus.summary: These tree-dwelling mosquitoes have found an unusual home that few others dare colonize: the hollow spaces and water pockets inside living trees. Unlike cousins that breed in standing water on the ground, they complete their entire life cycle in tiny tree holes, plant crevices, and rain-filled stumps—a strategy that makes them remarkably difficult to control with conventional mosquito management. Closely related to the notorious Asian tiger mosquito, they prefer the cooler climates of Europe and North Africa, quietly thriving in their arboreal nurseries where they're often completely overlooked.

Scolopendra cingulata.summary: As the most dominant banded centipede across the entire Mediterranean region, this species has earned its place as nature's heavyweight champion. The striking bands running along its body make it immediately unmistakable, and its remarkable success across diverse terrain speaks to exceptional adaptability. Found everywhere from sea-level coastal areas to inland mountain regions, it handles temperature swings and environmental challenges that would defeat most other centipede species. This is the Mediterranean's undisputed centipede champion, thriving where others merely survive.

Scolopendra heros.summary: This desert giant reaches several inches in length, earning its place among the largest centipedes in North America. With multiple regional names—Texas redheaded, Arizona desert, and giant Sonoran centipede—its reputation spans the Southwestern United States and Northern Mexico. What makes it truly impressive isn't just size, but its powerful venom and predatory prowess, capable of taking down creatures far larger than most centipedes would dare attempt. The combination of speed, strength, and toxin makes this creature a genuine apex invertebrate predator. Respect from considerable distance is highly recommended.

Scolopocryptops sexspinosus.summary: Don't let its modest 7-centimeter length fool you—this swift hunter moves with impressive speed and agility across the eastern forest floor. The eastern red centipede is surprisingly venomous for its size, inflicting genuinely painful bites that are dangerously underestimated by unsuspecting humans. Found throughout eastern North America beneath leaf litter and in soil, it represents one of the region's most capable midsized predators, hunting efficiently through darkness. Despite its smaller stature compared to giant cousins, this species is genuinely formidable and deserves respect.

Scolopendra viridis.summary: This striking tropical giant gets its distinctive blue coloration from specialized pigments, making it one of the more visually memorable centipedes in warm regions worldwide. Part of a remarkably diverse genus containing over a hundred different species of large tropical centipedes, this species represents an evolutionary triumph in design for invertebrate predation. These powerful tropical hunters are formidable enough to take down prey creatures many times larger than what smaller centipedes could pursue, armed with venom potent enough to match their considerable ambitions and voracious appetites.

Hemiscolopendra marginata.summary: Here's something genuinely remarkable: this species is the first centipede ever discovered to have different venom compositions between males and females—a trait called sexual dimorphism that was completely unknown in centipedes before its discovery. These bark specialists shelter beneath loose bark on dead or fallen trees, hunting in the protective darkness of their wooden homes. Found across the Eastern United States and into Mexico, they've perfectly adapted to exploit the hidden resources beneath bark, suggesting they've been evolving in isolation longer than most other centipede species.

Scolopendra polymorpha.summary: Talk about a world traveler—this desert specialist appears across western North America, Hawaii, Southern Africa, and beyond, making it one of the most widely distributed centipedes on Earth. Its many common names—tiger centipede, Sonoran Desert centipede, and banded desert centipede—reflect how different cultures recognize its success. The dramatic banding pattern helps it disappear into desert rocks and soil, a master of camouflage perfectly adapted to arid terrain. Wherever dry climates exist, this centipede seems to find its way and thrive.

Scolopendra subspinipes.summary: If any centipede deserves the title of world traveler, it's this one. Found across southeastern Asia, the Indian Ocean, tropical and subtropical Asia, Australia, the Caribbean, and possibly the southern United States, this massive species appears almost everywhere warm. With at least six different common names—black flame, jungle, Vietnamese, and orange-legged centipede—reflecting its stunning color variations, it's genuinely cosmopolitan. Scientists remain uncertain whether its range is entirely natural or partly due to accidental human transport, making it a geographic mystery wrapped in an evolutionary riddle.

Scolopendra morsitans.summary: Known as the Tanzanian blue ringleg or red-headed centipede, this species holds special significance in centipede science: it's the type specimen—the reference species against which all other Scolopendra are scientifically classified. This distinction makes it one of the most scientifically important centipedes on Earth, even if few have heard of it. The striking combination of red head and blue legs makes visual identification straightforward, and the species' importance in taxonomy ensures it will remain studied by arachnologists for generations. It's a humble but crucial creature in science.

Ethmostigmus trigonopodus.summary: Africa's giant centipede lives up to its fearsome reputation, and the distinctive blue rings adorning its legs advertise potent venom to potential threats. Also called the Tanzanian blue ring centipede (confusingly sharing that name with another giant), this species represents one of Africa's most formidable invertebrate apex predators. The combination of sheer size, striking coloration, and bright warning rings makes this one of the most visually arresting and immediately recognizable centipedes on the African continent—a creature that commands genuine respect and wariness.

Philoscia muscorum.summary:
Don't let the name fool you—these little crustaceans earned the nickname 'fast woodlouse' because they're speedsters in the woodlouse world. While most of their relatives plod along at a snail's pace, this species zips around with surprising agility, making it an efficient scavenger in damp forests and gardens.

Originally a European native, this species has become a globe-trotter, establishing itself across North America from Washington state to Nova Scotia. Quite the relocation for a creature barely bigger than a pencil eraser! You'll find them in woodpiles, under bark, and in leaf litter wherever it's moist and dark. These harmless decomposers do important cleanup work in ecosystems on both sides of the Atlantic.
